records.ety: [AS. wedd; akin to OFries. wed, OD. wedde, OHG, wetti, G. wette a wager, Icel. veð a pledge, Sw. vad a wager, an appeal, Goth. wadi a pledge, Lith. vadti to redeem (a pledge), LL. vadium, L. vas, vadis, bail, security, vadimonium security, and Gr. <?/, <?/ a prize. Cf. Athlete, Gage a pledge, Wage.]

quotes: Let him be ware, his neck lieth to wed [i. e., for a security].

records.ety: [OE. wedden, AS. weddian to covenant, promise, to wed, marry; akin to OFries. weddia to promise, D. wedden to wager, to bet, G. wetten, Icel. veðja, Dan. vedde, Sw. vädja to appeal, Goth. gawadjōn to betroth. See Wed, n.]
records.morph: [imp. Wedded; p. p. Wedded or Wed; p. pr. & vb. n. Wedding.]

defs: To take for husband or for wife by a formal ceremony; to marry; to espouse.
quotes: With this ring I thee wed.
I saw thee first, and wedded thee.

defs: To join in marriage; to give in wedlock.
quotes: And Adam, wedded to another Eve, Shall live with her.

defs: Fig.: To unite as if by the affections or the bond of marriage; to attach firmly or indissolubly.
quotes: Men are wedded to their lusts.
[Flowers] are wedded thus, like beauty to old age.

defs: To take to one's self and support; to espouse.
quotes: They positively and concernedly wedded his cause.
